text:
Nice! I love how you can follow how the code is working with the results of the calculations in the code itself and the visualisations. I wonder what's custom built for this example and what works for any use-case.

Also, is this new environment somewhere available to try out?

elements.text:
Nice! I love how you can follow how the code is working with the results of the calculations in the code itself and the visualisations. I wonder what's custom built for this example and what works for any use-case.

Also, is this new environment somewhere available to try out?

text:
Thanks Daniel!

I use Observable Framework, and its reactive JS. It doesn't have many mechanisms to encapsulate its own reactive code but I can consolidate common bits like the layout, editor, devtools in a `TEMPLATE.md` file that I include in other ("custom") OF md files: in this case `raycasting.md`

To show the workings like that we need to know values to use for every input, so there is a stateful 'cursor' object that custom code must set; also a stateful array of visible formulae that it can set if it wants to. The cursor object is handy anyways because custom code uses it to to make its visualizations/etc anyways.

`raycasting.md` custom code is dense on code for the visualizations: basically everything on the right hand side, and that includes signal handlers on the visualizations which use setters for the cursor object to set inputs, visible formulas to select formulas, and button controls to set a key stream input to the cursor; also those options at the bottom right.

That code includes approx 1k lines of visualization code (Vega-Lite for the scene visual at top, much denser Vega for different layers of the visualization below it)

If the app (lets call the RHS an "app") wants to control the cursor (=> the workings) and navigate the formulas then it needs to tie the handling of that into signal/event listeners.

That can be in a 'far less' elaborate way, for instance in this example: <https://calculang-editables.netlify.app/savings> (an earlier one where you can't see the workings on top of code, but they are calcd underneath)
This has another feature that highlight the numbers (in the grid) used to calc a number also (just brush over them).
And this is a bit more standard and I can facilitate a good co-operation in the views with less custom code.

In the example in the video though - there's some code to manage the cursor and formulae we are navigating (but I think we need to expect it)

There was a mash of experimentation in the codebase that gives it a poor structure and fragments work around calculang, so haven't released it yet but I'll think about it in the week

<https://open.substack.com/pub/duncancragg/p/from-spatial-to-parallel-reality?r=1sq2dz&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web>

attachments.title: From Spatial to Parallel Reality Computing

elements.text: Hiya, I've published another article, which says pretty much the same as before, but consolidates some ideas and brings things up-to-date:

text:
I asked Kagi summarizer this question:

> Imagine you built a chat system in this Parallel Reality, with the elements of a time-ordered sequence of messages that can have paragraphs and images created and posted by multiple users, all linked. how would hosting of each element of that be handled?
And, I have to say, Kagi "gets it":

In the *Parallel Reality* computing system described, the hosting and linking of the elements in a chat system would be handled as follows:

1. *Messages*: Each message would be a 3D world object, composed of linked paragraphs and images. These message objects would be hosted symmetrically across the devices of the users participating in the chat.
2. *Paragraphs and Images*: The individual paragraphs and images within each message would also be 3D world objects, linked to the message object. These atomic content elements could be hosted on any of the user devices participating in the chat, or even on separate servers.
3. *Linking*: All of these chat elements - messages, paragraphs, images - would be linked together using the advanced 3D linking system of the Parallel Reality. This allows users to seamlessly navigate, reference, and interact with the chat content, regardless of where the individual elements are hosted.
4. *Permissions*: Each user would have control over the read and write permissions for the world objects they have hosted on their local device. This allows for fine-grained control over who can access and modify the chat content.
5. *Synchronization*: The Parallel Reality operating system would handle the synchronization of the chat content across all participating devices, ensuring a seamless and consistent experience for all users.
In summary, the decentralized and linked nature of the Parallel Reality allows for a chat system where the individual elements are hosted flexibly across the user devices and servers, while still providing a cohesive and interactive experience through the advanced 3D linking capabilities.

text:
I released this calculang "environment" and it's code to GitLab:
<https://next-calculang-gallery.netlify.app/> The readme on GitLab has more info on what this is trying to be (and not be).
In a new example you can check the logic for a calculang Fizz Buzz :slightly_smiling_face:
